name: Citadel of Aleppo
shortDescription: One of the oldest and largest citadels in the world, rising above ancient Aleppo.
description: The Citadel of Aleppo sits on a massive tell that has been occupied since the third millennium BC. The current medieval fortifications — massive sloping glacis, monumental entrance tower, and inner palace — were built primarily under the Ayyubid sultan Ghazi I in the early 13th century. The 50-metre-high glacis of fitted stone blocks is among the most impressive defensive works in the Islamic world.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of Ancient City of Aleppo)

name: Citadel of Cairo
shortDescription: Saladin's citadel dominating Cairo — seat of Egypt's rulers for 700 years.
description: Built by Saladin (Salah ad-Din) in 1176 as part of a massive fortification project for Cairo and its surroundings, the Citadel of Cairo served as the seat of Egypt's government for nearly 700 years. Within its walls rose the great Mosque of Muhammad Ali (Alabaster Mosque), completed in 1848 and still the dominant landmark of the Cairo skyline. Multiple palaces, mosques, and halls were added by Mamluk and Ottoman sultans.
heritageStatus: UNESCO World Heritage Site (part of Historic Cairo)

name: Citadel of Qaitbay
shortDescription: The Mamluk fortress built on the site of the ancient Pharos of Alexandria.
description: Sultan Qaitbay built this sea fortress in 1477 on the exact site of the famous ancient Lighthouse of Alexandria — one of the Seven Wonders of the World. Many of its stones are believed to incorporate blocks from the lighthouse. Guarding the Eastern Harbour, it protected Alexandria from Ottoman naval attacks. The iconic square keep surrounded by sea walls is one of Egypt's most recognisable landmarks.

name: Belvoir Fortress
shortDescription: The finest Crusader concentric castle, commanding the Jordan Valley from a 500m plateau.
description: Belvoir, known in Hebrew as Kokhav HaYarden ('Star of Jordan'), is the best-preserved Crusader castle in Israel. Built by the Knights Hospitaller from 1168 and designed as a perfect double concentric rectangle, it controlled the Jordan Valley crossing and posed a constant threat to Saladin's communications. After a 1.5-year siege in 1189–1191, the garrison negotiated a honourable surrender and marched out with full honours.

name: İshak Paşa Palace
shortDescription: A remote Anatolian palace-fortress blending Ottoman, Armenian, Persian, and Georgian styles.
description: Built by the Cildirogullari dynasty of semi-autonomous Ottoman governors near the ancient city of Doğubayazıt, İshak Paşa Palace took nearly 100 years to complete. Its extraordinary hilltop location near the Iranian border, at 1,900 metres altitude with views of Mount Ararat, and its eclectic mixture of Seljuk, Ottoman, Georgian, and Iranian architectural styles make it one of the most remarkable palace-fortresses in the Middle East.

name: Kerak Castle
shortDescription: A mighty Crusader hilltop castle that resisted Saladin for years.
description: Built by the Crusaders in 1142 on a strategic ridge in Transjordan, Kerak controlled the road between Damascus and Egypt. Its most notorious lord, Raynald of Châtillon, used it as a base for raids on Muslim caravans and pilgrim routes, provoking Saladin. After the Crusader defeat at Hattin in 1187, Saladin besieged Kerak — the last major Crusader stronghold east of the Jordan — and took it in 1189.

name: Alhambra
shortDescription: The last great Moorish citadel-palace — the ultimate expression of Islamic art and architecture.
description: The Alhambra is a palace-fortress complex on a hill above Granada, built by the Nasrid dynasty as their royal capital in the 13th–14th centuries. Within its defensive walls lies some of the world's greatest Islamic art: the Court of the Lions, the Hall of the Ambassadors, and the Generalife gardens. The 'Red Fortress' fell to Ferdinand and Isabella in 1492, ending the last Moorish kingdom in Spain.

name: Alamut Castle
shortDescription: The eagle's nest fortress of the Assassins, held impregnable for 166 years.
description: Alamut — 'Eagle's Nest' in the Daylami language — sits on a sheer-sided rock in the Alborz Mountains of northern Iran. From 1090, it served as the headquarters of Hassan-i Sabbah and the Nizari Ismaili sect, popularly known as the Assassins. For 166 years it held out against Sunni powers until the Mongols under Hulagu Khan destroyed it in 1256. The library and observatory were lost; only ruins remain.
